Welcome back to What I=E2=80=99m Hearing, still tired from a looooong Oscar=
s weekend. Thanks to everyone
who waved hello or said nice things about What I=E2=80=99m Hearing or The T=
own, especially as I was stuffing my face with sushi and chicken pot pie at=
 the Governors Ball last night. It was great to meet so many thoughtful mem=
bers of the WIH community.

Tonight, the annual Awards Season Awards tradition continues. Plus a chat w=
ith Anora distributor Tom Quinn, whose phone pops up as =E2=80=9CKing of Os=
cars.=E2=80=9D For the next year, at least, nobody can tell him he=E2=80=99=
s
wrong.

=F0=9F=9A=A8=F0=9F=9A=A8 Oscar ratings contest update: Preliminary Nielsen =
numbers put Sunday=E2=80=99s show at 18 million viewers, down 7 percent fro=
m last year despite the Hulu bump (as glitchy as it was). I=E2=80=99m gonna=
 hold off on awarding the Puck merch and Wicked doll until we get final vie=
wership, so look for it on Thursday.

also said that the =E2=80=9Ceight-figure=E2=80=9D haul on home entertainmen=
t, before Anora hits Hulu on March 17, will compensate for that =E2=80=9Cer=
osion on theatrical,=E2=80=9D not to mention the value of Anora in the Neon=
 library. Below, I=E2=80=99ve included some highlights from Tom=E2=80=99s c=
hat with me and Bloomberg=E2=80=99s Lucas Shaw, edited for length and clari=
ty.

On the impact of a best picture win on a film and a 60-employee indie distr=
ibutor=E2=80=A6

=E2=80=9CThere are multiple bumps.
Post-noms, if you=E2=80=99re a best picture nominee, whether or not you=E2=
=80=99re on home entertainment, there=E2=80=99s still a huge bump in box of=
fice. But that box office bump has decreased, I think significantly, post p=
andemic. The bump for the last three best picture noms that we=E2=80=99ve h=
ad=E2=80=94Triangle of Sadness, Anatomy of a Fall, and [Anora]=E2=80=94have=
 been massive on home entertainment. This will get into the eight-figures s=
um [on P.V.O.D. and electronic sales before it goes to Hulu].=E2=80=9D

How Neon was able to get U.S. rights to =E2=80=98Anora=E2=80=99 in 2023, de=
spite Baker=E2=80=99s two previous films, =E2=80=98The Florida Project=E2=
=80=99 and =E2=80=98Red Rocket,=E2=80=99 both going to archrival A24=E2=80=
=A6

=E2=80=9CWe desperately wanted to work on Florida Project, made an offer at=
 the last minute, and lost it to A24. We came pretty close on Red Rocket, b=
ut the reality is that we=E2=80=99ve had this longstanding relationship wit=
h Sean because Sean is the single biggest lover of cinema. He
supports us in our movies. So we had a shot at this one. And I think having=
 lost the last two, this was not something we intended on losing. I got the=
 script, and with Glen Basner [of Film Nation], it was a quick, 24-hour neg=
otiation.=E2=80=9D

On Baker=E2=80=99s Oscar speech advocating for theatrical distribution=E2=
=80=A6

=E2=80=9COne of the telltale signs of movies that matter is box office. I p=
ersonally don=E2=80=99t believe that you can be a best picture contender if=
 you
gross less than $10 million and/or you don=E2=80=99t report box office.=E2=
=80=9D

On the cost of the =E2=80=98Anora=E2=80=99 campaign=E2=80=A6

=E2=80=9CIt=E2=80=99s under $20 million. It=E2=80=99s a big bet, but it was=
 important for us to do everything we could to live up to that potential.=
=E2=80=9D

On the impact of the changing Academy, with more international voters, and =
younger and more diverse voters=E2=80=A6

=E2=80=9COnly 20 percent of the total voters are international, so it=E2=80=
=99s
a swing vote. I think what=E2=80=99s more important is that all the new vot=
ers are aggressively and excitedly participating in these Academy campaigns=
=2E They=E2=80=99re more engaged. I thi=
nk they=E2=80=99re watching everything=
=2E The rise of some of the independent=
 Oscar contenders post-Parasite is att=
ributable not just to the international voter, but to the new voters.=E2=80=
=9D

On the relatively low box office of winners like =E2=80=98Anora,=E2=80=99 =
=E2=80=98The Brutalist,=E2=80=99 =E2=80=98The Substance,=E2=80=99 and even
=E2=80=98Conclave=E2=80=99...

=E2=80=9CListen, we=E2=80=99re one of the smallest companies in that lineup=
 of best picture. And so the idea that we sit at the same table as Warner B=
ros. and Universal=E2=80=A6 we can compete at a high level, but we can also=
 be more creative, take more risks with the films that we believe will reso=
nate more than a movie with a budget and a creative backdrop that serves a =
studio of 9,000 employees.=E2=80=9D

How he intends to win Baker=E2=80=99s next
movie=E2=80=A6

=E2=80=9CWe would finance and produce more than just his next film. And it =
really honestly has nothing to do with the fact that he won best picture. H=
e represents the values and the kind of filmmaking that this company has be=
en built on.=E2=80=9D

But what if Sean says he wants to do a $100 million movie, like Bong Joon H=
o did with =E2=80=98Mickey 17=E2=80=99 after =E2=80=98Parasite=E2=80=99? Wa=
rner Bros. took that bet=E2=80=A6

=E2=80=9CWe will not
be in that game. And honestly, I wouldn=E2=80=99t understand a budget of a =
film over $35 million. There are other people who do that better than us.=
=E2=80=9D

=E2=80=8A

The
Feedback
--------

I got tons of funny responses to Thursday=E2=80=99s whole Kathy Kennedy
blowup, but I=E2=80=99m done with that silliness. Instead, some thoughtful =
takes from Oscar voters on my critique of the Academy=E2=80=99s internation=
al push=E2=80=A6

=E2=80=9CTotally agree with the assessment of AMPAS. It=E2=80=99s not the N=
BA. [Other countries] all have their own =E2=80=98leagues=E2=80=99 with the=
 Goyas, et al. We didn=E2=80=99t need to represent international filmmaking=
 beyond the category and allowing those films to compete elsewhere. The sho=
w is going to close the door on the ratings, go to Netflix
(I hear this over and over from inside), and we=E2=80=99ll never know how m=
any viewers it actually gets because the board and [C.E.O.] Bill [Kramer] d=
on=E2=80=99t give a shit. They only, understandably, give a shit about the =
income=E2=80=94whatever the results might foretell.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94An Ac=
ademy member

=E2=80=9COne way to make the Oscars more connected to the American moviegoi=
ng public: Create an exhibition branch. Long overdue.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94An
executive

=E2=80=9CI think less of an issue than the internationalization has been th=
e lowering of the bar for who gets admitted. And no branch has been more eg=
regious than the documentary branch. Something gets lost when you start add=
ing festival programmers, film critics, film fund financier executives, and=
 Netflix buyers into that AMPAS mix. There is a different agenda beyond jus=
t craft.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94A documentary filmmaker=C2=A0

=E2=80=9CThey need to
require proof of you having watched the movies to vote in the final round. =
Either you attended a screening in person or proof that you played through =
the full movie on the app. It is embarrassing that so many of these anonymo=
us voters are saying they never bothered to watch the 10 [best picture nomi=
nees]. And, anecdotally, so many abstained from voting for smaller categori=
es, or are saying they didn=E2=80=99t watch many of the nominated titles at=
 all.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94Another executive

=E2=80=8A

Have a great week,
Matt

=80=99re on home entertainment, there=E2=80=99s still a huge bump in box of=
fice. But that box office bump has decreased, I think <em>significantly</em=
>, post pandemic. The bump for the last three best picture noms that we=E2=
=80=99ve had=E2=80=94<em>Triangle of Sadness</em>, <em>Anatomy of a Fall</e=
m>, and [<em>Anora</em>]=E2=80=94have been massive on home entertainment. T=
his will get into the eight-figures sum [on P.V.O.D. and electronic sales b=
efore it goes to Hulu].=E2=80=9D
<br/><br/><em>How Neon was able to get U.S. rights to =E2=80=98Anora=E2=80=
=99 in 2023, despite Baker=E2=80=99s two previous films, =E2=80=98The Flori=
da Project=E2=80=99 and =E2=80=98Red Rocket,=E2=80=99 both going to archriv=
al A24=E2=80=A6</em><em><br/></em><em><br/></em>=E2=80=9CWe desperately wan=
ted to work on <em>Florida Project</em>, made an offer at the last minute, =
and lost it to A24. We came pretty close on <em>Red Rocket</em>, but the re=
ality is that we=E2=80=99ve had this longstanding relationship with Sean be=
cause Sean is the single biggest lover of cinema. He
supports us in our movies. So we had a shot at this one. And I think having=
 lost the last two, this was not something we intended on losing. I got the=
 script, and with <strong>Glen Basner</strong> [of Film Nation], it was a q=
uick, 24-hour negotiation.=E2=80=9D <br/><br/><em>On Baker=E2=80=99s Oscar =
speech advocating for theatrical distribution=E2=80=A6</em><em><br/></em><e=
m><br/></em>=E2=80=9COne of the telltale signs of movies that matter is box=
 office. I personally don=E2=80=99t believe that you can be a best picture =
contender if you
gross less than $10 million and/or you don=E2=80=99t report box office.=E2=
=80=9D <br/><br/><em>On the cost of the =E2=80=98Anora=E2=80=99 campaign=E2=
=80=A6</em><em><br/></em><em><br/></em>=E2=80=9CIt=E2=80=99s under $20 mill=
ion. It=E2=80=99s a big bet, but it was important for us to do everything w=
e could to live up to that potential.=E2=80=9D <br/><br/><em>On the impact =
of the changing Academy, with more international voters, and younger and mo=
re diverse voters=E2=80=A6</em><em><br/></em><em><br/></em>=E2=80=9COnly 20=
 percent of the total voters are international, so it=E2=80=99s
a swing vote. I think what=E2=80=99s more important is that <em>all</em> th=
e new voters are aggressively and excitedly participating in these Academy =
campaigns. They=E2=80=99re more engaged. I think they=E2=80=99re watching e=
verything. The rise of some of the independent Oscar contenders post-<em>Pa=
rasite</em> is attributable not just to the international voter, but to the=
 <em>new</em> voters.=E2=80=9D<br/><br/><em>On the relatively low box offic=
e of winners like =E2=80=98Anora,=E2=80=99 =E2=80=98The Brutalist,=E2=80=99=
 =E2=80=98The Substance,=E2=80=99 and even
=E2=80=98Conclave=E2=80=99...</em><em><br/></em><em><br/></em>=E2=80=9CList=
en, we=E2=80=99re one of the smallest companies in that lineup of best pict=
ure. And so the idea that we sit at the same table as Warner Bros. and Univ=
ersal=E2=80=A6 we <em>can</em> compete at a high level, but we can also be =
more creative, take more risks with the films that we believe will resonate=
 more than a movie with a budget and a creative backdrop that serves a stud=
io of 9,000 employees.=E2=80=9D<br/><br/><em>How he intends to win Baker=E2=
=80=99s next
movie=E2=80=A6</em><em><br/></em><em><br/></em>=E2=80=9CWe would finance an=
d produce more than just his next film. And it really honestly has nothing =
to do with the fact that he won best picture. He represents the values and =
the kind of filmmaking that this company has been built on.=E2=80=9D<br/><b=
r/><em>But what if Sean says he wants to do a $100 million movie, like </em=
><strong><em>Bong Joon Ho </em></strong><em>did with =E2=80=98Mickey 17=E2=
=80=99 after =E2=80=98Parasite=E2=80=99? Warner Bros. took that bet=E2=80=
=A6</em><em><br/></em><em><br/></em>=E2=80=9CWe will not
be in that game. And honestly, I wouldn=E2=80=99t understand a budget of a =
film over $35 million. There are other people who do that better than us.=
=E2=80=9D</p></div>

edy</em></strong><em>
blowup, but I=E2=80=99m done with that silliness. Instead, some thoughtful =
takes from Oscar voters on my critique of the Academy=E2=80=99s internation=
al push=E2=80=A6</em><em><br/></em><em><br/></em>=E2=80=9CTotally agree wit=
h the assessment of AMPAS. It=E2=80=99s not the NBA. [Other countries] all =
have their own =E2=80=98leagues=E2=80=99 with the Goyas, <em>et al</em>. We=
 didn=E2=80=99t need to represent international filmmaking beyond the categ=
ory and allowing those films to compete elsewhere. The show is going to clo=
se the door on the ratings, go to Netflix
(I hear this over and over from inside), and we=E2=80=99ll never know how m=
any viewers it actually gets because the board and [C.E.O.] <strong>Bill</s=
trong> [<strong>Kramer</strong>] don=E2=80=99t give a shit. They only, unde=
rstandably, give a shit about the income=E2=80=94whatever the results might=
 foretell.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94<em>An Academy member</em><em><br/></em><em><b=
r/></em>=E2=80=9COne way to make the Oscars more connected to the American =
moviegoing public: Create an exhibition branch. Long overdue.=E2=80=9D =E2=
=80=94<em>An
executive</em><em><br/></em><em><br/></em>=E2=80=9CI think less of an issue=
 than the internationalization has been the lowering of the bar for who get=
s admitted. And no branch has been more egregious than the documentary bran=
ch. Something gets lost when you start adding festival programmers, film cr=
itics, film fund financier executives, and Netflix buyers into that AMPAS m=
ix. There is a different agenda beyond just craft.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94<em>A =
documentary filmmaker=C2=A0 </em><em><br/></em><em><br/></em>=E2=80=9CThey =
need to
require proof of you having watched the movies to vote in the final round. =
Either you attended a screening in person or proof that you played through =
the full movie on the app. It is embarrassing that so many of these anonymo=
us voters are saying they never bothered to watch the 10 [best picture nomi=
nees]. And, anecdotally, so many abstained from voting for smaller categori=
es, or are saying they didn=E2=80=99t watch many of the nominated titles at=
 all.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94<em>Another executive</em></p></div>
